speaker: interviewer
text: I will ring the bell. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: You-- [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: Your keys and the--

speaker: interviewer
text: I got my keys.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And [INAUDIBLE] does-- really can open lightly the door.

speaker: interviewer
text: He should have been [INTERPOSING VOICES] knock.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very lightly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just quietly, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Quietly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just so we don't disturb the interview.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: The date is March 14, 1995. The survivor is Harriet Solz, interviewer Sherry Amatenstein in New York, USA. The language is English. I'm Sherry Amatenstein, A-M-A-T-E-N-S-T-E-I-N. It's March 14, 1995, and this is an interview with Harriet Solz. And we're in-- we're in Forest Hills, New York, in the USA. May I ask you to say your name and to spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Harriet Solz, S-O-L-Z.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your name at birth, and how do you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: O-F-F-M-A-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any other spellings, or nicknames, anything like that you were known by?

speaker: interviewee
text: By my Polish name, first name. Henia. H-E-N-I-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was your birthday?

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now I'm 65.

speaker: interviewer
text: And also, the-- those two. Say the city, your place of birth, and to spell that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Krakow, Poland. K-R-A-K-O-W.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your pre-war home life? Like did you-- how large was your family? [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, I was 10 years old when the war broke out to begin with. And I had a younger sister, so-- and my parents. We were four people in the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your sister's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hermina. And I had a beautiful life as a child. I really didn't know from nothing. I went to dancing school, I took piano lessons, I went to the movies, went to the circus.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, and that's-- when I was a, a real-- how shall I say? I was a big baby--

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] --at that time because I was-- I came from a very strict home where the upbringing was very strict and sheltered life. I wasn't allowed to go to a park by myself. Even to school, the maid had to walk me to take me to school and pick me up from school, and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say that you were spoiled, in what way?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wasn't spoiled, I was sheltered I would say more. Spoiled I wasn't because I had very strict upbringing. The manners were very important in our house, table manners. And-- only sheltered. I wasn't-- I didn't have the freedom, which some children my age already had that freedom. Alone to play in the streets, and so on, I wasn't allowed. I was a baby. [LAUGHS] A baby.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of work did your family do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father had a business, led a business. And my mother never worked.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a store or factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: A store. A store, With leather.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you spend time in the store?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I used to come every day for five-- to get $0.05 for candies. [LAUGHS] That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] What language did you speak inside your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about outside the home? What-- where you learning other languages as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, when I went to school, which my career in school wasn't too long, I was learning Hebrew. But otherwise, we spoke Polish a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you when you started going to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think I was six years old, if I'm not mistake. I know when they closed the schools for Jewish children, I started fourth grade, public school.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember of school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, school. What I remember-- first, I went to public school. And then my parents took me out, and put me in a private school. I don't know why they did it. This I cannot tell you. And I-- that private school was called Hebrew School, which I had difficulties with the Hebrew language, and I had to have a tutor to help me with it. But nothing special, nothing really.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the public school, were there non-Jewish children as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the private school, no. That was strictly Jewish school.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the first one though?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any memories of, of that at all? Playing with children or any interactions with any of the non-Jewish children?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really don't recall it because I was never left alone. Like I said, the maids used to take me to school and pick me up. And then I had a next-door neighbor, which we played together. And I don't recall any-- with non-Jewish children, for sure not because it was such a big anti-Semitism that we wouldn't mingle. Either side wouldn't mingle. And I-- I remember I went to the park skiing, so they broke my ski because I was Jewish. This was before the war. And I used to go with my father-- he used to take me ice skating-- to a private, Jewish ice skating rink. And when the war broke out, they closed up the Jewish ice skating rink, so he took me to a mixed one, Gentiles and Jews. So, me, I look always typical Jewish. They almost broke my legs on the skating rink. Here is a Jew skating, and this was my last experience of skating.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember how you felt when that happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: How it felt? Terrible. But we grew up in this atmosphere that the Jews are hated in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what your parents used to say to you about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't remember. But we were mostly with Jewish people, Jewish relatives.

speaker: interviewer
text: How large was Krakow? [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I hear-- I don't remember Krakow too well. Like I said, I was too young, and I never went by myself, only with my parents for a walk. And even when we went with my parents for a walk, me and my sister held hands together like that. And we walk in front of the parents when we went to the parks, something like that. But I hear had 80,000 Jews, Krakow, in a quarter million, I think, population. But I'm not sure, don't quote me.

speaker: interviewer
text: How religious was your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were modern observant. We observe all of the holidays, and-- but we had a kosher home. But otherwise, we were very modern.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went only on high holidays. My father took me for the shofar. But like this-- and there was reform temple, Which my mother sometimes used to take me for services. But I never went. You see, the Hebrew school, school was modern. Wasn't religious, but was modern.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sorts of rituals did you observe in the home?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a kosher home, like I said. I observe all the holidays. And I actually was more religious than the whole household because my neighbor was religious, so I became religious on my own. [LAUGHS]. That's when I got sick. My father wanted to take a cab. Not a cab. We had the horses like on Fifth Avenue in Central Park. So I said, I won't to ride on Saturday.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he says, if you're sick, you're allowed. I said no, and he had to carry me maybe for a mile or longer to take me home. I was the one religious, really. Do you have any memories of the Jewish holidays besides your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah. The Passover, we always had the beautiful seder, which my-- my father was very traditional. When came holidays, you would think he's very, very religious. Everything was performed to a T. But after the holidays, he went back to-- [LAUGHS] --to his ways. So all of the holidays we celebrate and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's your earliest memory as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: My earliest memory. I really don't know what to say was my earliest memory.

speaker: interviewer
text: A happy memory from before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah, oh yeah. Happy family. Like I said, sheltered child, going to dressmakers to have dresses made up. And going to family's for holidays, for dinners. And playing with dolls. I had a Shirley Temple doll already then, which was a big thing in Poland. So I really had a good life, going to-- for summer vacation. We used to go away, every summer away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] We went, the name [NON-ENGLISH] that was a resort, [BACKGROUND NOISE] and we went a few times there Then we went to [NON-ENGLISH] another resort. And as a matter of fact, before the war broke out, I was never happy about going away from Krakow. For me was-- I never liked to leave Krakow. As a matter of fact, I supposed to go to a camp overnight. 1939, supposed to go to camp, in the place, [NON-ENGLISH], something like-- I don't remember the name. But there were talks of the war, so my parents were afraid to send me. But I don't think I would have enjoyed it. I'm not the camping type [LAUGHS].

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you when the war broke out?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 10 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember anything about that day?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll tell you. I am ashamed to admit, but I'll tell you the honest truth. You know, a child mind works differently than grownups. And I have a granddaughter who is 10 years old, so I always watch her and compare. When I heard the war broke out, I was happy. It's a shame to admit because I used to study geography, and Poland was in other wars. So I thought I'll sit by the window, and I'll watch them fighting.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I remember when the war broke out. Later on, the soldiers would park in the park when they came in to Krakow. I ran to the park to look at them. And to me-- to me, this was-- I said, they're not so bad, what people were talking. But what did I know about? What did you hear and see that day? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing special, nothing special. But later on, it started. They started regular procedure with, with cutting down places where Jews can go-- can go. Not every place we were allowed to walk. And we had to wear armbands, Jewish armbands. But I didn't have to because I was too young. It started at age 13. But I was jealous because everybody wears a Jewish armband.

speaker: interviewer
text: See how a child mind [BACKGROUND NOISE] works? So I used to take from home the armband, and put-- once I left the house, I put it on my arm to walk around with it. What color was the armbands that you had?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- it was white. White with a blue Jewish star.

speaker: interviewer
text: What other-- you were talking about restriction. Was it a gradual [BACKGROUND NOISE] process or did it happen very quickly? Like all of a sudden, there were-- like your father, probably-- did they take away his store?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my father was in the army when the war broke out. And he-- but he came when the war finished. He was taken to prison, and then they released him as a prisoner, war prisoner. And they released him. He came to Krakow. Some once my father came, of course, the situation at home improved.

speaker: interviewer
text: How, how long was it until your father came back to Krakow?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. It took few weeks because when the war started, we stood in line for bread and everything. But my father came, and he came home with two big breads, which was a very big relief. And he was with us all the time. Then I would say the war broke out in September. October, November they closed schools for Jewish children. Jewish children weren't allowed to go to school. Me again, I was happy. I won't have to go to school. I'm ashamed to admit, but that's the way I felt. But my-- to my father, education was the most important thing. And he organized with few other parents who have children the same age when we went to school. And we were studying in hiding. If they would have caught us, they would have killed us. And we were maybe five or six girls, which were studying. And we had three professors on different subjects. And I, I completed public school because, like I would say, within two, three months, the course completed in a way. So I completed public school. And this went through to ghetto and the first year of gymnasium. And I remember my father went to see the teacher, how I am doing. So he says I don't know if I'll promote your daughter to second year gymnasium because she's very bad in Latin. She's falling. [LAUGHS] Failing. In those times, in such a-- [LAUGHS] But the education was taken serious. So I was-- I know this. So I have to study, other children don't have to. But in the long run, he did the right thing. I, I never realized what this means.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your parents scared? Did you hear them talking?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah. They were scared about us, because me and my sister, we were very young. And children were taken away. Children were killed. And they always shivered. What's going to happen to us?

speaker: interviewer
text: And Krakow was the capital of-- it was designated the capital of the government. There must-- what do you remember of the German soldiers being there?

speaker: interviewee
text: German soldier. That-- first of all, whenever they've had their chance, they try to kill somebody. And especially religious Jews, they used to pull out the locks, the side locks with the skin. And we used to have to clean the streets, snow off the streets. And in Poland, we have lot of snow. And, and the struggle what was going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the color of their uniforms or anything like that? When you'd see the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: They had-- they had black, which was SS. They had yellow, which was SA called. They were the murders. And then the army uniform, green. Green color.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember at all when action started? Roundups?

speaker: interviewee
text: Action started? I recall it first in ghetto, really. Their actions, the real actions of-- they-- and we knew about that, as they come and people were on the list. At night, they picked them up. They took them to Auschwitz. But we were still in our houses in Krakow at that time. And then they started. We have to bring our furs. Whoever had the fur, had to bring to the main market to, to give up the furs, and so on. But their actions, as I recall it, started in ghetto, what I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was the ghetto established in Krakow?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1941. And that time, we went to ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Like Spielberg made a fantastic scene showing people leaving Krakow. We dragged our belongings with us. We thought, God knows what we going to-- and we'll be able to keep whatever you were able to pack up and push on the-- on the carts. You pushed the belongings with you, and you took it to ghetto. But in the meanwhile, we came to ghetto, the facilities weren't what were-- what you were used to. The apartments. At the beginning, we got one room with a kitchen for four people, which was a luxury. So you had no room. My mother, I remember, took her paintings with her to hang up, but there was no room where to do it. In ghetto, really, start all the actions. And constantly, they, they came to the houses, check. And luckily, I was tall for my age, so I got away. Which I-- we had false papers, me and my sister made, as we are much older than we were.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when the-- at the beginning, they used to look at your papers, the IDs. So we got away. Sometimes we were hidden under the beds because depends on what kind of action was going on. How frequent were these?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were quite often, really quite often. Big actions. And most of the people went. They took them to [NON-ENGLISH] And from there, they would send them to Belzec, to [INAUDIBLE] Belzec.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember how you got the papers? Did your parents get them for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- my parents got for me. I wasn't-- I couldn't get it for my own. I wouldn't know even what to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your name? Your, your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah. This, everything I remembered. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name on the papers? Your false names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the same names.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh just the-- just the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Polish name, what was on my birth certificate, which was Henrika. And that's the name. Only the dates are changed.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the dates changed to?

speaker: interviewee
text: To-- that's the best-- I saw the I didn't remember that, and I never knew. My sister was born in 1931, and I was born in 1929. So we are both marked born 1927, within two weeks [LAUGHS]. I was born May 1, she was born May 15. I have the list. But-- and you live constantly in the fear. You didn't know what next day going to bring. You didn't think that you're going to live next day.

speaker: interviewer
text: You felt that as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah. And I tell you, I am left still today with so much fear in my body that you can't imagine. I was always very scared, very unsure of myself. And I thought, maybe if I would be more mature, I would feel differently. But very scared. And to today's day, when I see policeman, I'm scared. I can't walk in, like, to a bank. My husband has to go with me. Of any official offices, I can't walk. I get anxiety at this. I can't walk in, I'm frightened. And I am frightened to sign any legal papers, to put my name to it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your father working in the ghetto? What was day-to-day life like?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father-- for awhile, it was-- since we had the leather business, they combined all the leather stores, and they made one big store. So my father was managing-like, and he was going out of the ghetto to work. Since he was going out of the ghetto, even though always something was able to smuggle in to the-- to the house. So he was working for awhile in-- outside the ghetto in Krakow. But later on, in Krakow-- I mean, in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you still getting schooling at all in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: In ghetto? Yeah. I still was getting till the when they had the less action, which was brutal. Lot of people were killed and, and sent out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was for awhile in the ghetto. We were from 1941 through '43 in ghetto. So I would say for a year, maybe, was receiving schooling. Were you were about to celebrate Jewish holidays at all in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't recall it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there barbed wired? Was there--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, there were barbed wires, no question about it. And the ghetto was getting, every day, smaller and smaller. We lived with my aunt, and uncle and their children. We're, maybe, 10 people in one room at the end. And everybody had a nail hanging over their bed to know-- to have room where to put their clothes because in the morning, they shouldn't look for it. The conditions were terrible. And the toilets were outside. I don't know how we managed that. [LAUGHS]. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: And so many people. How did you get food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Food-- I think they gave us rations.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember being hungry a lot?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah, oh yeah. Sure, I remember being hungry and everything. But still again, I was stupid child things that's going to--

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were in ghetto, ghetto B, actually. And ghetto A was for people who were able to work. Ghetto B was for families with children. Which later on, this was designed to go to Auschwitz to the ovens. But when they were liquidating ghetto, my father was already in Plaszow. How, what? Don't ask me, I don't know. But he was in Plaszow. And I remember my mother was packing again those big--

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] --big straw baskets to take to-- that's because they said we're going to take you to a nice place. They didn't say they going to take us to the ovens. And we were in ghetto B where people didn't work there. There were old people and children.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my father came, smuggle himself into ghetto. He came running, and he pulled us out from ghetto B in the last minute. He was in Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was already in Plaszow.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did he get out? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Smuggled. People smuggled ways. What?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, keep--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They-- you know, people tried to smuggle through, finagle. You'd be surprised how people used to. They smuggle in children in suitcases going to, to Plaszow. Because children weren't allowed to go, so they put them in knapsacks and suitcases. You became shrewd. You had to fight for your life.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my father smuggled himself through. Of course, he risked his life. Everything, whatever you did, you risked your life. If it went through, fine, You were OK, you were a hero. So he pulls us from ghetto B in the last minute. And he dressed us up in my mother coats, long coats with high heels, that we should look older, they shouldn't stop us at the gate going to Plaszow because Goth was watching every face. Goth was the biggest murderer in Plaszow. So me, such a spoiled child, I went to take-- you wouldn't believe it worked-- a strainer. What is a strainer? In Poland, you boiled milk, so there was always a skin on top of the milk a skin foam from boiling. And I wouldn't drink milk if the skin was floating. So I had my personal strainer over a glass, which you put it, and you poured the milk through so whole skin was left on the strainer. I took with me to Plaszow in case I get milk. How will I be able to drink it?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: How far was Plaszow from Krakow?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think couple miles. I don't remember. It was a long walk, but how long, far, I really don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know how many people were going at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. Maybe 20,000 or more. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was 1943 when the Krakow ghetto was liquidated?

speaker: interviewee
text: March 13, yeah. They liquidated Krakow. So Saturday, we went. They took us-- we managed to get to Plaszow.
